title: Closing the Loop
subtitle: Remediation, follow-up & audit readiness

hook.metaphor: Getting a diagnosis is worthless if you never fill the prescription. A check-up that finds a problem you then ignore is arguably worse than no check-up — now you *knew* and did nothing. The real payoff of audit isn't the report; it's the loop being closed: the issue fixed, and someone confirming it actually got fixed.
hook.scenario: Last year's audit found the same three issues that *this* year's audit just found again. Management 'accepted' the findings both times but never actually fixed them. The audit function is working perfectly — so why is nothing getting better?

learn: An audit finding's life isn't over when it's reported — it needs **remediation**: a concrete fix, with a named **owner** and a **deadline** (the same discipline as Internal Controls). 'Management accepted the finding' is not a fix; acceptance without action is exactly our repeat-finding trap.

Then comes **follow-up**: audit checks back to confirm the fix was actually implemented *and works* — not just that someone ticked 'done'. **Repeat findings** (the same issue, audit after audit) are a red flag that the loop isn't closing, and a sign of weak accountability or culture, not weak auditing.

Sometimes management makes a conscious choice to **accept the risk** rather than fix it — which is legitimate *if* it's a deliberate, documented decision by someone with the authority and risk appetite to own it. The danger is fake acceptance: 'accepting' a risk simply to avoid the work, with no real sign-off (our scenario).

From the auditee's side, this is where **audit readiness** pays off: keeping evidence organised year-round so audits are smooth, and — more importantly — running a tight ship so there's little to find. The best way to ace an audit is to not need heroics for it (the 'controls that last' lesson, again).

Underpinning it all is the **audit committee** of the board, which tracks open findings to closure and holds management accountable. Without that top-level pressure, findings quietly rot — and the loop our scenario needed was board-level follow-through, not better fieldwork.
bridge: So at work, you can spot whether audit actually *works* by looking past the report to the loop: *are findings fixed with owners and deadlines, does someone verify the fix, and are repeat findings being chased down?* An organisation that keeps re-discovering the same issues doesn't have an audit problem — it has an accountability problem, and that's where to push.

interactions.title: The same three findings, again
interactions.prompt: This year's audit re-found last year's three issues; management 'accepted' them both times but never fixed them. The real problem?

options.label: The auditors aren't finding new things.
options.feedback: The auditors did their job — they found the issues, twice. The breakdown is afterwards: no remediation, no follow-up, no accountability. That's not an audit problem.

options.label: The loop isn't closing — findings accepted but never remediated or followed up.
options.feedback: Exactly. Acceptance isn't a fix. Repeat findings signal weak accountability and culture; the missing piece is owned remediation, follow-up verification, and board pressure to close.

options.label: Stop auditing those areas to avoid repeat findings.
options.feedback: That just hides the problem. The fix is to close the loop on the known issues, not to stop looking at them.

interactions.title: Closing the loop or not?
interactions.prompt: Sort each action by whether it genuinely closes the audit loop.

items.label: Assigning a fix to an owner with a deadline
Audit re-testing to confirm the fix actually works
'Management accepted the finding' with no action
The audit committee tracking findings to closure
Marking a finding 'done' without verifying it

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “An audit only pays off when the loop closes — findings get fixed with owners and deadlines, audit verifies the fix worked, and repeat findings get chased — otherwise you just keep re-diagnosing the same untreated illness.”

checkpoint.q: 'Repeat findings' (the same issue audit after audit) usually signal…
options: Weak accountability — the loop isn't closing on known issues
checkpoint.explain: Finding it twice means it was never remediated — an accountability/culture problem, not an audit one.

options: Verifying the agreed fix was actually implemented and works
checkpoint.explain: Confirming the remediation truly happened — not just that someone ticked 'done'.

checkpoint.q: Consciously 'accepting' an audit risk is legitimate only when it's…
options: A way to dodge the work quietly
A deliberate, documented decision by someone with the authority and appetite to own it
checkpoint.explain: Genuine risk acceptance is owned and signed off; fake acceptance to avoid effort is the trap.
